annotations.label: Simpson's paradox

anchor.snippet: a phenomenon in probability and statistics in which a trend appears in several groups of data but disappears or reverses when the groups are combined

annotations.note: No definition of Simpson's paradox appears in Mathlib; grep for `Simpson` returns only unrelated references to Alex Simpson in topology.

annotations.label: UC Berkeley gender bias

anchor.snippet: One of the best-known examples of Simpson's paradox comes from a study of gender bias among graduate school admissions

annotations.note: Real-world dataset examples are not in scope for Mathlib and no formalization exists.

annotations.label: Kidney stone treatment

anchor.snippet: Another example comes from a real-life medical study

annotations.note: Empirical medical-study example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Batting averages of Jeter and Justice

anchor.snippet: A common example of Simpson's paradox involves the batting averages of players in professional baseball

annotations.note: No baseball-statistics example is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vector interpretation of Simpson's paradox

anchor.snippet: Simpson's paradox can also be illustrated using a 2-dimensional vector space

annotations.note: No vector-slope interpretation of Simpson's paradox is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Correlation reversal from lurking confounder

anchor.snippet: Simpson's reversal can also arise in correlations

annotations.note: Grep for `confounder`/`confounding` returns no hits; no formalization of correlation reversal exists.

annotations.label: Qualified Savage sure-thing principle

anchor.snippet: An action A that increases the probability of an event B in each subpopulation

annotations.note: Savage's sure-thing principle is not stated or proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hadjicostas probability for 2x2x2 tables

anchor.snippet: in a random 2 × 2 × 2 table with uniform distribution, Simpson's paradox will occur with a probability of exactly

annotations.note: Hadjicostas's exact probability computation for 2×2×2 tables is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kock's probability for path models

anchor.snippet: the probability that Simpson's paradox would occur at random in path models

annotations.note: Kock's path-model probability result is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simpson's second paradox

anchor.snippet: A second, less well-known paradox was also discussed in Simpson's 1951 paper

annotations.note: Simpson's second paradox is not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Back-door criterion

anchor.snippet: the partitioning variables must satisfy a graphical condition called "back-door criterion"

annotations.note: Grep for `back.?door` returns no hits; Pearl's back-door criterion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjustment formula via back-door criterion

anchor.snippet: When the back-door criterion is satisfied by a set Z of covariates, the adjustment formula

annotations.note: The causal adjustment (do-calculus) formula is not stated in Mathlib.
